answers.value:
We are requesting a neighborhood traffic speed and volume study for North Phelps Avenue between Alabama Drive and Mizell Avenue due to persistent speeding, cut-through traffic, and safety concerns for children and pedestrians.

Over the past two years, many of us have noticed a significant increase in drivers using North Phelps Avenue as a shortcut between Fairbanks Avenue and the medical complex near Mizell, particularly during morning and late afternoon rush hours. Although the posted speed limit is 25 mph, it is common to see vehicles traveling closer to 35–40 mph, especially downhill sections and when drivers are trying to beat the light at Mizell. Several neighbors have informally tracked this by timing cars between driveways and using consumer-grade speed gun apps, and while we understand those are not official measurements, they consistently show speeds well above the limit.

We have a high concentration of young families, retirees, and dog walkers on this stretch of Phelps. There are no dedicated bike lanes and only intermittent sidewalks; in some places, people have to walk along the edge of the roadway or cross mid-block to avoid parked cars. In the last six months alone, there have been at least three near-miss incidents where a speeding vehicle had to brake hard to avoid a child on a bicycle or someone backing out of a driveway. One neighbor’s mailbox has been hit twice, and another neighbor’s parked car mirror was clipped by a passing truck that did not stop.

Visibility is also an issue. Many of the driveways along this segment back directly onto the street with limited sight distance because of mature trees and on-street parking. When cars are traveling at or below the speed limit this is manageable, but at higher speeds it becomes very difficult to safely back out. Several older residents have expressed that they now avoid driving at certain times of day because they do not feel comfortable entering the roadway when traffic is heavy and fast.

We are not requesting any specific traffic calming device at this time; rather, we are asking the City of Winter Park to conduct a formal traffic speed and volume study to quantify the problem and help us understand what options might be appropriate (e.g., speed humps, additional signage, striping, or other measures). We would also appreciate professional guidance on whether existing signage is sufficient and if crosswalk enhancements are warranted near the informal pedestrian crossing mid-block.

In summary, our primary concerns are: (1) frequent speeding above the posted limit, (2) increased cut-through traffic during peak hours, (3) safety risks to children walking and biking to nearby parks and schools, and (4) difficulty for residents entering and exiting their driveways safely. We believe a formal study will provide the data needed for a thoughtful, collaborative solution between residents and the City.
